nativeRange.habitat: The Painted Sweetlips is reef-associated and tends to favour open muddy, sandy, or silty substrates in protected bays and estuaries, around rock outcrops, wreckage, and rubble, as well as on shallow coastal coral reefs. It is generally found at depths of roughly 1 to 50 m, though it has been recorded as deep as approximately 170 m. It is often encountered in turbid water, singly or in small groups, and juveniles tend to shelter in weedy zones.

decorPreferences: live rock arranged into caves and ledges for daytime shelter

diet.notes: In the wild, the Painted Sweetlips has been documented feeding on benthic invertebrates — crustaceans, mollusks, and worms — as well as small fishes, foraging primarily at night. In captivity, newly imported specimens are commonly slow to accept food and may need to be enticed with live saltwater shrimp, or freshwater ghost shrimp as a temporary substitute. Once established, individuals generally accept a varied carnivore diet of chopped shrimp, squid, clam, mussel, krill, mysis, marine fish flesh, and prepared frozen carnivore foods. Multiple small meaty feedings per day are typically recommended given the species' eventual large size and rapid growth.

avoidWith: very small fish that could be swallowed
highly aggressive tankmates that stress juveniles

summary: The Painted Sweetlips (Diagramma pictum) is a peaceful but large Indo-Pacific grunt with one of the most striking juvenile-to-adult color transformations in marine fishkeeping. Adults commonly reach around 55 cm and can approach 100 cm, making this species suited only to very large fish-only systems.
careNotes:
The Painted Sweetlips is a large Indo-Pacific member of the family Haemulidae (the grunts and sweetlips), also known in the trade under the older names Plectorhinchus pictus and Spilotichthys pictus. It is generally considered peaceful toward tankmates of comparable size but is recommended only for expert keepers due to its eventual size and rapid growth rate. Adults have been reported to reach about 100 cm total length, with a common length of around 55 cm, making the species impractical for all but the largest private aquariums and public displays — a tank of at least 2,000 litres with a minimum length of 300 cm is generally advised.

The species undergoes one of the most dramatic juvenile-to-adult color changes in marine fishkeeping. Juveniles display bold alternating black-and-white stripes with silvery-yellow underparts and yellow fins marked with black blotches. Adults shift to a predominantly blue-grey body covered in bright yellow to golden-orange spots that form line patterns over the head, with a vivid orange-red mouth interior. Prospective keepers should be aware that the small striped juvenile typically offered for sale will look entirely different at adult size.

Feeding is the most commonly cited husbandry challenge. Like other sweetlips, newly imported specimens are often slow or reluctant to feed and have been known to require live shrimp to initiate a feeding response. Once eating reliably, individuals generally transition to a varied frozen and prepared meaty diet. Multiple small feedings per day are commonly advised given the species' rapid growth.

Reef compatibility is best treated with caution: the Painted Sweetlips is not aggressive toward tankmates of comparable size but will hunt smaller crustaceans, worms, and small fishes consistent with its wild diet, making it generally unsuitable for typical reef setups. Fish-only-with-live-rock systems featuring open swimming space and caves or ledges for daytime shelter are the preferred arrangement. The species is largely nocturnal in the wild, foraging over open substrate at night. Like other members of the family Haemulidae, it produces audible grunting sounds by grinding its pharyngeal teeth — the internal set of teeth located in the throat — which is how the grunts get their common name.
breedingNotes: The Painted Sweetlips is oviparous, with distinct pairing reported during spawning in the wild, and sexual maturity is reached at around 31.8 cm. Captive reproduction has not been documented in typical home aquaria, and is generally considered impractical outside specialised public-aquarium settings given the adult size and demanding husbandry.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Painted Sweetlips product page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/painted-sweetlips) provided scientific name Plectorhynchus pictus (an older synonym of Diagramma pictum), origin Indo-Pacific, listed max size 8 in (overview prose contradicts with 'up to 33 inches'), minimum tank 50 gal, care level Easy, temperament Peaceful, reef compatible Yes, diet Carnivore (krill/mysis/zooplankton/brine shrimp/crustaceans), temperature 72-78°F (22.2-25.6°C), pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.020-1.025, and notes nocturnal bottom-feeder that disturbs substrate and threatens small fish/invertebrates.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed family Haemulidae, order Eupercaria/misc, max size 100 cm FL (common 55 cm), depth 1-50 m (recorded to ~170 m), preferred temperature 24.1-29.1°C (mean 28°C), maximum reported age 13 years, Indo-West Pacific distribution (Arabian Sea/Mozambique to Palau, Japan to New Caledonia), diet benthic invertebrates and fishes, oviparous with distinct pairing during spawning, marine/reef-associated.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Painted sweetlips)
additional.notes: Cross-checked size, range, and color phases: max length 100 cm (typical 55 cm, max weight 6.3 kg), Indo-Pacific from Red Sea/KwaZulu-Natal east to Fiji and north to Japan, depth to 80 m, juveniles black-and-white striped vs adults blue-grey with golden-yellow spots and orange-red mouth, diet benthic invertebrates and smaller fishes, distinct pairing during spawning, synonyms Perca picta/Plectorhinchus pictus/Spilotichthys pictus. Used in lieu of the playbook's first listed secondary (Reef2Reef wiki) which has historically returned HTTP 403 to WebFetch from this slice.

fish.breedingNotes: Wild spawning is oviparous with distinct pairing; captive reproduction has not been documented for typical home aquaria given adult size and feeding requirements.
